1: Isaiah 45:1 opens the Cyrus oracle proper: 'ko-amar YHWH limshicho leKhoresh, thus says YHWH to his anointed, to Cyrus.' The mashiach (anointed = the Hebrew term for messiah, used for Israelite kings anointed with oil at coronation: 1 Samuel 10:1; Isaiah 16:13; 2 Samuel 2:4; and for priests: Exodus 29:7; Leviticus 4:3) applied to a gentile pagan king is unprecedented in the OT — Cyrus is the only non-Israelite ever called YHWH's mashiach. This does not make Cyrus the Messiah in the NT sense, but it does show YHWH's sovereign authority to commission and anoint any king, regardless of religion. 'Whose right hand he has grasped to subdue nations before him' (asher-chazaqti biyminoh = whose right hand I have grasped) = the same hand-grasping of 41:13 for Israel. Babylon's gates falling for Cyrus was historical fact: the Babylonian Chronicle records that Cyrus's general Ugbaru entered Babylon without battle, and Cyrus himself entered the city shortly after.
2: 'I will go before you and make the rough places smooth; I will break the gates of bronze in pieces and cut through the bars of iron' — the divine escort: 'ani lefanekha elek vehadorim ayashsher = I before you will go and the rough/swelling places I will level.' The divine warrior going before his instrument: the same imagery as Isaiah 40:3 (preparing YHWH's way) now applied to preparing Cyrus's way. 'The gates of bronze' (daltot nechoshet = bronze doors): Herodotus (1.179) describes Babylon's inner gateways as having bronze gates — 'bronze gates' became proverbial for Babylon's impregnability. 'Bars of iron' (uvrichei barzel = and bars of iron) = the iron locking-bars that secured the massive gates. The historical account: Cyrus diverted the Euphrates and entered under the bronze water-gates — the famous gates literally were bypassed from below.
3: 'I will give you the treasures stored in darkness and the wealth hoarded in secret places, so that you may know that I, the LORD, am the God of Israel who has called you by name' — the divine enrichment of Cyrus and its missionary purpose: 'venatatti lekha otsarot choshekh = and I will give you the treasures of darkness.' The treasures of darkness (otsarot choshekh = treasuries in the dark = the hidden vaults of the Babylonian temples and palace storehouses) — historically, Babylon's temples contained enormous wealth accumulated from centuries of tribute and plunder (including the treasures from Jerusalem's temple, 2 Kings 24:13; Isaiah 25:13-17). The purpose: 'lema'an teda ki ani YHWH = so that you may know that I, YHWH.' Even Cyrus, who did not know YHWH (v4: 'though you did not know me'), is given material evidence of who directed his career.
4: 'For the sake of my servant Jacob, and Israel my chosen, I called you by your name; I gave you a title of honor, though you did not know me' — the motivation for Cyrus's divine appointment: 'lema'an avdi Ya'aqov veYisrael bechiiri = for the sake of my servant Jacob and Israel my chosen one.' YHWH's use of Cyrus is not because Cyrus deserves it or even because Cyrus is aware of it — but entirely 'for the sake of Jacob.' 'I gave you a title of honor, though you did not know me' (va'akunnekha velo yedatani = and I surnamed/titled you, though you did not know me) — the kinnah (title/surname/honorific = a formal designation of identity and role) given to Cyrus despite his ignorance of YHWH. This is the most explicit statement of divine sovereignty using a non-believing instrument in the OT: YHWH's purposes are accomplished through those who don't recognize him as the one directing them.
5: 'I am the LORD, and there is no other; besides me there is no God. I equipped you, though you did not know me' — the repeated monotheistic assertion: 'ani YHWH ve'en od = I, YHWH, and there is no other.' The en od (there is no other = not merely 'no other god' but 'no other' of any kind in the divine category) = the absolute monotheism. 'Besides me there is no God' (mibbal'adai en Elohim = apart from me there is no God). 'I equipped you' (va'azarezekha = I girded/equipped you, from azar = to gird for strength or battle) — YHWH equipped Cyrus for his campaigns. The repeated 'though you did not know me' (velo yedatani) = the divine freedom to act through the unknowing: YHWH's purposes are not limited by the awareness or faith of his instruments.
6: 'So that from the rising of the sun to where it sets, people may know there is no one besides me; I am the LORD, and there is no other' — the universal-knowledge purpose of the Cyrus-deployment: 'lema'an yede'u mimizrach-shemesh = so that they may know from the rising of the sun.' The entire inhabited world ('from the rising to the setting of the sun' = the complete east-west span) is the target audience of the Cyrus-demonstration. YHWH deploys Cyrus not merely to save Israel but to demonstrate his uniqueness to the entire world. The missionary dimension of Israel's rescue: when YHWH saves his people through an impossible means (a pagan emperor), the watching world learns who YHWH is. The 'may know' (yede'u = the covenant-knowing of experiential recognition) is the purpose of all YHWH's historical acts throughout Isaiah 40-55.
7: 'I form light and create darkness; I make peace and create disaster; I, the LORD, do all these things' — the most theologically challenging verse of Isaiah 45: 'yotser or ubore choshekh oseh shalom ubore ra = forming light and creating darkness, making peace and creating evil/disaster.' The Hebrew ra (evil/disaster = both moral evil and catastrophic calamity; in context of YHWH's speech this is best read as calamitous disaster/adversity rather than moral evil). The verse is structured as an anti-dualism polemic: Persian Zoroastrianism posited two co-eternal, co-equal deities — Ahura Mazda (the good principle, creator of light) and Angra Mainyu (the evil principle, creator of darkness) locked in eternal conflict. YHWH's claim: he is the sole ultimate cause of both light/darkness and peace/adversity. There is no second divine principle. This does not make YHWH the author of moral evil but asserts that even calamity is within his sovereign control and purpose.
8: 'Rain down, O heavens, from above, and let the skies pour down righteousness; let the earth open up and salvation spring forth, and let righteousness grow up with it; I, the LORD, have created it' — the prayer for the new-creation outpouring: 'harziyfu shamayim mima'al = rain down, O heavens from above.' The heavens are invited to rain (harziyfu = the intensive form of razaf = to drip/pour) their tsedaqah (righteousness = the covenant-right-ordering of all relationships). 'Let the earth open up and salvation spring forth' (vitetpetach erets veyiphreru yesha = and let the earth open and let salvation bear fruit) — salvation as crop growing from rain-watered soil. 'And let righteousness grow up with it' (utsedaqah titsmach yachdav = and righteousness spring up together). The meteorological-agricultural imagery: righteousness falls from heaven, salvation grows from earth — a vertical and horizontal integration of divine gift. This verse underlies John 3:16's gift-from-above theology and the parable of the sower (Matthew 13:3-9).
9: 'Woe to him who quarrels with his Maker — a mere potsherd among potsherds of earth! Does the clay say to the one who shapes it, What are you making? or Your work has no handles?' — the woe against challenging the divine use of Cyrus: 'hoy rib et-yotsro = woe to the one who strives with his maker.' The hoi (woe = the prophetic grief-cry that introduces a judgment oracle: Isaiah 1:4; Isaiah 5:8-23; Isaiah 10:1) against the one who riv (disputes/quarrels with = the same legal term as the divine courtroom disputations of Isaiah 41-44) his Maker. 'A mere potsherd among potsherds of earth' (cheres et-charshe adamah = a shard among the shards of the earth) — the human being who disputes with YHWH is merely a piece of broken pottery disputing with the potter. 'Does the clay say to the one who shapes it, What are you making?' (hayomer chomer leyotsero mah-taaseh = will the clay say to its potter: what are you making?) — the absurdity of the creature questioning the creator's design (Jeremiah 18:4-6; Romans 9:20-21: 'Does the pot say to the potter: Why did you make me like this?').
10: 'Woe to him who says to a father, What are you fathering? or to a woman, What are you in labor with?' — the second woe extending the creator-creature analogy to parenthood: 'hoy omer le'av mah-tolid = woe to the one who says to a father: what are you fathering?' The father-child and mother-child relationships as analogies for YHWH-Israel: just as a child cannot dictate the circumstances of its own birth, Israel cannot dictate to YHWH how he accomplishes Israel's redemption (through Cyrus). 'Or to a woman, What are you in labor with?' (vela'isha mah-techalin = and to the woman: what are you giving birth to?) — the birth-labor analogy continues the childbirth imagery of Isaiah 42:14 (YHWH crying out like a woman in labor). The parent-child relationship as the ground of both divine authority over the redemption-plan and divine commitment to the child's existence and flourishing.
11: 'Thus says the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, and his Maker: Will you question me about my children, or give me orders about the work of my hands?' — YHWH's direct response to the objectors: 'ko-amar YHWH qedosh Yisrael veyotsero = thus says YHWH, the Holy One of Israel, and his Maker.' The dual title (Holy One of Israel + Maker) responds to both the 'potter' and 'parent' analogies: YHWH is the maker/craftsman of Israel (potter) and the parent. 'Will you question me about my children?' (ha'otiyot shealuni = will you ask me about the things to come?) — the objectors who question YHWH's use of Cyrus are questioning the divine parent's choices about his own children. 'Or give me orders about the work of my hands?' (uveal-me'aseh yadai tetsavvuni = and about the work of my hands will you command me?) — the creature cannot command the Creator about his own design.
12: 'I made the earth and created mankind upon it; with my own hands I stretched out the heavens, and I commanded all their starry host' — the Creator's credentials: 'anoki asiti erets ve'adam aleyhah barati = I made the earth and mankind on it I created.' Three creation-acts: (1) made the earth; (2) created mankind on it; (3) 'ani yadai nateu shamayim = my own hands stretched out the heavens.' The 'I commanded all their starry host' (vekhol-tseva'am tsivviti = and all their host I commanded) — the stars as YHWH's army under his direct command (Isaiah 40:26: 'he leads out their host by number, calling each by name'). The contrast with Babylonian astrology that treated the stars as independent divine powers governing fate: YHWH commands the stars; they do not command him or the course of history.
13: 'I have stirred him up in righteousness, and I will make all his paths straight; he shall build my city and set my exiles free — not for price or reward, declares the LORD of hosts' — the righteous commission of Cyrus: 'ani ha'irotiv bitsedaqah = I have stirred him up in righteousness/for the right purpose.' The stirring up in tsedaqah (righteousness = for the sake of covenant right-ordering; not 'for Cyrus's personal righteousness' but 'for my righteous purpose'). 'He shall build my city' (hu-yivneh iri = he will build my city = Jerusalem) and 'set my exiles free' (galuti yeshalech = he will release my exile/exiles). Historically: Cyrus's edict (538 BCE, Ezra 1:1-4) decreed the return of the Jewish exiles and the rebuilding of the Jerusalem temple. 'Not for price or reward' (lo vemechir velo vishochad = not for payment and not for a bribe) — Cyrus freed Israel without demanding tribute or personal payment, which is historically accurate: the Cyrus Cylinder shows him as presenting himself as a liberator, not an extractor.
14: 'Thus says the LORD: The wealth of Egypt and the merchandise of Cush, and the Sabeans — tall men — shall come over to you and be yours; they shall follow you; they shall come over in chains and bow down to you, pleading, Surely God is in you, and there is no other, no god besides him' — the nations' recognition oracle: 'yegi Mitsrayim usechar Kush = the toil/tribute of Egypt and the merchandise of Cush.' Egypt (Mitsrayim), Cush (Ethiopia), and the Sabeans (shevan'im = the South Arabian tribe of Sheba, known for trade in gold, spices, and precious stones, 1 Kings 10:1-13; Ezekiel 27:22) = the three great trading civilizations of the south, representing the wealth of the known world. 'Come over in chains and bow down to you' (ya'avoru velaykh yihyu = they will come over and to you they will belong; vealayikh yishtaghu = and to you they will bow) — not chains of conquest but possibly the chains of tribute-bearing submission. The climax: 'akh bekha El ve'en od = surely in you is God, and there is no other.' The Gentile recognition formula: seeing YHWH's act through Israel, the nations confess what Israel had claimed all along.
15: 'Truly you are a God who conceals himself, O God of Israel, the Savior' — the deus absconditus: 'aken attah El mistatter Elohei Yisrael moshi'a = truly you are a God who hides himself, O God of Israel, the Savior.' The aken (truly/surely = the affirmation that follows on evidence) + El mistatter (a God who hides/conceals himself = from sathar = to hide, conceal; Psalms 10:11: 'he hides his face and never sees'). The hester panim (hiding of the face) was the OT's theological term for divine absence during disaster and exile (Psalms 13:1; Isaiah 88:14; Isaiah 8:17). The paradox: the hidden God is the Savior God. The concealment is not absence but the invisibility of the divine action working through Cyrus — only retrospectively visible as YHWH's hand. Martin Luther built his theology of the cross on this verse: God is most hidden (crucified) when he is most powerfully saving.
16: 'They are all put to shame and confounded; together all the makers of idols go off in disgrace' — the contrast: idol-makers shamed vs. Israel saved: 'bosh veham kol-hem yachdav halekhu vekhalimat choreshei tselilim = they will be ashamed and also confounded, all of them together; they will go in disgrace, the craftsmen of idols.' The makers of images go off (halekhu = they went, the perfect of certainty) in disgrace (vekhalimat = and with disgrace/confusion = the shame of public failure). The contrast with Israel in v17 is precisely drawn: idol-makers → shame/disgrace; Israel → everlasting salvation/never put to shame.
17: 'But Israel is saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation; you will not be ashamed or confounded for ages everlasting' — the everlasting-salvation promise: 'Yisrael noshia beLYHWH teshu'at olamim = Israel is saved by YHWH with salvation of eternities.' The olamim (eternities/ages = plural of olam = the strong form of the permanent unending character of the salvation) — this is not merely temporal rescue from Babylon but permanent covenant-status restoration. 'You will not be ashamed or confounded for ages everlasting' (lo tevoshu velo tikkalemu ad-olmei ad = you will not be shamed and will not be confounded unto the ages of eternity). The double negative (lo...velo) = emphatic: not at all ashamed, not at all confounded. The antithesis to the shame of the exile and the shame of the idol-makers (v16): what they experience permanently (shame), Israel will not experience again.
18: 'For thus says the LORD, who created the heavens — he is God — who formed the earth and made it, who established it and did not create it a chaos but formed it to be inhabited: I am the LORD, and there is no other' — the creation-purpose statement: 'ki ko-amar YHWH bore hashamayim hu ha'Elohim = for thus says YHWH, the creator of the heavens, he is the God.' The creation-credential: (1) bore hashamayim (creator of the heavens); (2) yotser ha'arets va'asah (former and maker of the earth); (3) hu khonenah (he established it); (4) lo-tohu bera'ah lashevet yetsarah (he did not create it tohu/chaos but formed it for inhabitation). The theological point: YHWH's creational purpose is order and habitation (lashevet = for sitting/dwelling/inhabiting), not chaos (tohu = the Genesis 1:2 void). The exile (Israel driven from its land = dispossession and disorder) contradicts YHWH's creational intent — which is exactly why the restoration is necessary and certain: YHWH's creative purpose cannot be permanently frustrated.
19: 'I did not speak in secret, in a land of darkness; I did not say to the offspring of Jacob, Seek me in a waste. I, the LORD, speak truth; I declare things that are right' — YHWH's transparent revelation contrasted with pagan oracle-systems: 'lo beseter dibarti bemaqom erets choshekh = I did not speak in secret, in a place of dark earth.' The Babylonian divination system required elaborate ritual, initiated specialists, and secret knowledge to interpret divine communication. Greek oracles (Delphi) were famously ambiguous. YHWH's self-contrast: his word is spoken in the open, not in a land of darkness or chaos. 'I did not say to the offspring of Jacob, Seek me in a waste' (lo amartiy lizera Ya'aqov tohu baqshuni = I did not say to the seed of Jacob: seek me in chaos) — YHWH's call is not to seek him in the tohu (chaos/waste) but in the ordered structure of covenant, Torah, and worship. Cf. John 18:20: 'I have spoken openly to the world... I said nothing in secret.'
20: 'Assemble and come; draw near together, you survivors of the nations. Those who carry around their wooden idols and pray to a god that cannot save have no understanding' — the universal invitation to the nations: 'hiqqavetsu uvo'u hitnaggoshu yachdav pleytei haggoyim = assemble and come; draw near together, survivors of the nations.' The peleytei haggoyim (survivors of the nations = those who have escaped the Cyrus-conquest, the remnant of the Babylonian empire's subject peoples) are invited to the divine court. 'Those who carry around their wooden idols' (hannose'im et-ets pitslam = those who carry around their carved wooden image) — the idol-carrying procession: in Babylon, the great Akitu festival featured the gods being carried in procession through the city. 'Pray to a god that cannot save' (vamitpallelim el-el lo yoshia = and praying to a god that cannot save) — the ultimate pragmatic test of deity: can it save (yasha)? The test YHWH will pass in v21-22.
21: 'Declare your case and present your arguments; let them consult together. Who foretold this from long ago? Who declared it from of old? Was it not I, the LORD? There is no other god besides me — a God who is righteous and saves; there is none besides me' — the courtroom verdict: 'haggidu vehaggishu af yio'atsu yachdav = declare and present; also let them consult together.' The nations' case invited and then dismissed: 'mi-hishmi'a zot miqedem = who made this heard from antiquity?' The prediction-test applied to Cyrus's fulfillment: who predicted all this? 'Was it not I, the LORD?' (halo anoki YHWH ve'en od Elohim = was it not I, YHWH, and there is no other God?). The verdict: 'El-tsaddiq umoshi'a en zulati = a righteous God and a Savior — there is none besides me.' The tsaddiq (righteous/just = the one who acts in accordance with covenant right-order) combined with moshi'a (Savior = the one who delivers) = YHWH is both just (the exile was just punishment) and saving (the restoration is genuine salvation).
22: 'Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth! For I am God, and there is no other' — the most explicit universal salvation-invitation in the OT: 'penu elai vehivashe'u kol-afsei-erets = turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth!' The penu (turn = the same root as teshuvah/repentance: turning from and turning toward) elai (to me = YHWH) directed to kol-afsei-erets (all the ends of the earth = the maximally inclusive geographic scope, encompassing every inhabited region). 'For I am God, and there is no other' (ki-ani El ve'en od = for I am God and there is nothing more/other) — the monotheistic ground for the universal invitation: there is no other deity to turn to, so the invitation extends to all. John 12:32: 'I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.' Matthew 28:19: 'Go and make disciples of all nations.'
23: 'I have sworn by myself; a righteous word has gone out from my mouth, and it will not turn back: To me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear allegiance' — the divine oath and the universal confession: 'bi nishbati = by myself I have sworn.' YHWH cannot swear by anything greater than himself (Hebrews 6:13: 'since there was no one greater for him to swear by, he swore by himself'), so the oath bi (by myself) = the highest possible guarantee. 'A righteous word has gone out from my mouth, and it will not turn back' (yatsa mippi tsedaqah davar velo yashuv = there has gone out from my mouth a word of righteousness and it will not return) — the irresistible prophetic word (Isaiah 55:11: 'my word that goes out from my mouth will not return to me empty'). 'To me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear allegiance' (kol-berekh tikra kol-lashon tishava = every knee will bow, every tongue will swear). Paul quotes this in Philippians 2:10-11 for the universal acknowledgment of Christ as Lord; Romans 14:11 for the divine judgment.
24: 'Only in the LORD, it will be said, are righteousness and strength; all who were angry with him will come to him and be ashamed' — the universal resolution: 'akh beLYHWH li yomer = only in YHWH, it will be said for me.' The akh (only/surely = the exclusive particle; righteousness and strength are found only in YHWH). 'Righteousness and strength' (tsedaqot va'oz = righteousnesses and strength) — the two divine attributes that the nations need and only YHWH provides: tsedaqot (the plural = the many righteous acts) and oz (the strength/power that backs them). 'All who were angry with him will come to him and be ashamed' (ve'edaiv yabo'u veiyevoshu kol-hacharim bo = and to him will come and be ashamed all those who were incensed against him) — the coming-to-shame is the recognition-in-shame of those who opposed YHWH: not their destruction but their conversion through the shame of realizing they were wrong.
25: 'In the LORD all the offspring of Israel shall be found righteous and shall exult' — the final promise: 'beLYHWH yitsdaqo veythehallelu kol-zera Yisrael = in YHWH all the offspring of Israel will be declared righteous and will glory/boast.' The yitsdaqo (will be declared/found righteous = from tsaddiq = the legal declaration of righteousness, justification) 'in YHWH' (beLYHWH = in YHWH, not in themselves) — Israel's righteousness is not self-achieved but found in YHWH. The yithehallelu (will boast/glory = from halal = praise; the boasting is praise-boasting, hallelujah-boasting) = the ultimate resolution: Israel vindicated before the nations, boasting not in their own merit but in YHWH. Jeremiah 9:24: 'let the one who boasts boast about this: that they understand and know me'; 1 Corinthians 1:31: 'let the one who boasts boast in the Lord.'
